skill.name: defuddle
skill.description: Extract clean markdown content from web pages using Defuddle CLI, removing clutter and navigation to save tokens. Use instead of WebFetch when the user provides a URL to read or analyze, for online documentation, articles, blog posts, or any standard web page.
skill.summary: Extract clean markdown content from web pages using Defuddle CLI, removing clutter and navigation to...
skill.author: kepano

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged shell backticks and weak cryptographic algorithms. The weak crypto alerts are false positives from text containing the word description, while the shell alerts reflect real external CLI instructions that fetch user-provided URLs and can write files. No malicious intent or prompt injection was found, but publication should include a warning about command execution and network access.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution With User-Provided URLs
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs agents to run Defuddle CLI against a user-provided URL. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it creates network access and command injection risk if the URL is passed through a shell without safe argument handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples directly accept <url> and invoke an external executable. The behavior is expected for this skill, so the risk is contextual rather than confirmed malicious.

medium_findings.title: Global Package Installation Instruction
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill tells users to install Defuddle globally with npm if it is not installed. Global installs can modify the host environment and run package lifecycle scripts, creating supply chain and persistence risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The npm global install instruction is explicit. It is not malicious by itself, but it expands trust to the external package and npm installation process.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Several static findings identify Markdown backticks, code fences, and table cells as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The file is Markdown documentation, so these markers do not execute by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced content is Markdown formatting and examples, not executable Ruby code. The semantic context is clear from the file type and surrounding headings.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography at the description text and the metadata property named description. No cryptographic API, algorithm selection, hashing, encryption, or cipher use appears in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only evidence is ordinary text containing description. There is no code or instruction that uses DES or any other weak cryptographic algorithm.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Invokes Network Fetching
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on defuddle parse <url>, which runs an external executable and fetches remote content. Agents should pass URLs as isolated command arguments and avoid shell interpolation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is explicit and central to the skill. The risk depends on runtime handling of user-provided URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Writes Output File
dangerous_patterns.description: The save example writes parsed web content to content.md. This is useful, but agents should avoid overwriting important files and should confirm target paths when needed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file output instruction is explicit. The example uses a fixed filename, which limits but does not remove overwrite risk.

content.value_statement: Web pages often include navigation, ads, and clutter that waste model context. This skill uses Defuddle CLI to extract cleaner Markdown, metadata, or saved content from standard URLs.
actual_capabilities: Runs Defuddle CLI to parse a URL into Markdown output.
Prefers Markdown output for cleaner model-readable page content.
Can save extracted Markdown to a local content.md file.
Can extract page metadata such as title, description, and domain.
Documents available output modes for Markdown, JSON, HTML, and metadata properties.
limitations: Requires the Defuddle CLI and npm installation access when the tool is missing.
Needs network access to fetch the target web page.
May not capture content behind authentication, paywalls, or heavy client-side rendering.
User-provided URLs must be handled carefully to avoid unsafe shell interpolation.

use_cases.title: Summarize an Article
use_cases.description: Extract the readable article body before asking the model to summarize or compare it.

use_cases.title: Read Online Documentation
use_cases.description: Convert documentation pages into Markdown so an agent can inspect APIs with less page clutter.

use_cases.title: Collect Page Metadata
use_cases.description: Retrieve a page title, description, or domain for citation review and source organization.

prompt_templates.title: Extract a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Defuddle to extract clean Markdown from this URL, then summarize the main points: [URL]

prompt_templates.title: Save Clean Content
prompt_templates.scenario: Store extracted text for later review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Defuddle to save the clean Markdown from this URL to a local file, then tell me what was captured: [URL]

prompt_templates.title: Check Metadata
prompt_templates.scenario: Review source identity before deeper analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Defuddle to get the title, description, and domain for this URL. Then explain whether the source matches my topic: [URL]

prompt_templates.title: Compare Multiple Sources
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract clean Markdown from these URLs with Defuddle. Compare their claims, note contradictions, and list source metadata for each page: [URLs]

output_examples.input: A user provides a long article URL and asks for the important points.
output_examples.output: The assistant extracts the readable article content, removes page clutter, and returns a concise summary with the main claims.

output_examples.input: A developer shares a documentation page and asks which options are supported.
output: The page title and domain are identified.
The relevant documentation text is extracted as Markdown.
The assistant lists the supported options in plain language.

output_examples.input: A content team needs metadata from a source page.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns the page title, description, and domain, then explains how the source can be cited or categorized.

best_practices: Use this skill for standard web pages, articles, blog posts, and public documentation.
Quote or pass URLs as separate command arguments when running the CLI.
Review extracted content against the source when accuracy or citations matter.
anti_patterns: Do not use it for private pages without permission or required authentication.
Do not paste untrusted URLs into a shell command without safe argument handling.
Do not assume the extracted Markdown includes every script-rendered page element.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides an agent to use Defuddle CLI to extract cleaner Markdown and metadata from web pages.

faq.question: When should I use it instead of WebFetch?
faq.answer: Use it when a standard web page has navigation, ads, or layout clutter that wastes context.

faq.question: Does it require installation?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill says to install Defuddle globally with npm if the CLI is not available.

faq.question: Can it save extracted content?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented example saves Markdown output to a local content.md file.

faq.question: Can it extract metadata only?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents metadata properties for title, description, and domain.

faq.question: What security warning applies?
faq.answer: It runs an external CLI against user-provided URLs, so URLs and output paths need careful handling.
